# TODOs
No TODOs yet, if you are working on tasks that would benefit from a todo list please use the todoWrite tool to create one.</system-reminder>

The repository at `/home/user/repo` has bookmarks `main`, `feature-a`, `feature-b`, and `feature-c` with overlapping ancestry. Write a script that uses `jj` revsets to analyze the ancestry relationships and output a report to `/home/user/ancestry_report.txt`. The report must cover: (1) commits in `feature-a` not reachable from `main`, (2) commits present in the ancestry of all three feature branches, (3) the merge base between `feature-a` and `feature-b`, and (4) commits that modified `src/core.py` across any feature branch.
Let me first explore the repository structure and understand the current state of the jj repository before writing the script.

node labeled "(elided revisions)".

The working-copy commit is indicated by a `@` symbol in the graph. [Immutable revisions] have a `◆`

--
          Typically useful for shell commands like: `jj diff -r @- --name-only | xargs perl -pi
          -e's/OLD/NEW/g`

      --git
          Show a Git-format diff

parts.text: Now I have a thorough understanding of the repository. Let me write the analysis script.

todos.content: Write the jj ancestry analysis script
Run the script and verify the output report

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# analyze_ancestry.sh
# Analyzes bookmark ancestry relationships in the jj repository and writes a
# structured report to /home/user/ancestry_report.txt.
#
# Revsets used:
#   main..feature-a          — commits reachable from feature-a but NOT from main
#   ancestors(X) & ancestors(Y) & ancestors(Z)
#                            — commits in the shared ancestry of all three branches
#   heads(ancestors(feature-a) & ancestors(feature-b))
#                            — merge-base between feature-a and feature-b
#   (main..feature-X) & file("src/core.py")
#                            — commits that touched src/core.py in each feature branch

set -euo pipefail

REPO_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
REPORT="/home/user/ancestry_report.txt"

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helper: run a jj log query and emit a numbered list of results.
# Usage: list_commits <revset> [<template>]
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
list_commits() {
    local revset="$1"
    local template="${2:-short_id ++ \" \" ++ if(description, description.first_line(), \"(no description)\") ++ \"\\n\"}"
    local results
    results=$(
        jj log \
            --no-graph \
            --no-pager \
            -T "$template" \
            -r "$revset" \
            2>&1
    )
    if [[ -z "$results" ]]; then
        echo "  (none)"
    else
        local n=1
        while IFS= read -r line; do
            [[ -z "$line" ]] && continue   # jj emits blank lines between entries
            printf "  %2d. %s\n" "$n" "$line"
            (( n++ ))
        done <<< "$results"
    fi
}

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helper: emit a section header.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
section() {
    local title="$1"
    printf '\n%s\n' "$(printf '%.0s=' {1..72})"
    printf ' %s\n' "$title"
    printf '%s\n' "$(printf '%.0s=' {1..72})"
}

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Collect metadata once so we can print it in the header.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
cd "$REPO_DIR"

MAIN_TIP=$(jj log --no-graph --no-pager -T 'short_id ++ "\n"' -r 'main' 2>&1 | head -1)
FA_TIP=$(jj log --no-graph --no-pager -T 'short_id ++ "\n"' -r 'feature-a' 2>&1 | head -1)
FB_TIP=$(jj log --no-graph --no-pager -T 'short_id ++ "\n"' -r 'feature-b' 2>&1 | head -1)
FC_TIP=$(jj log --no-graph --no-pager -T 'short_id ++ "\n"' -r 'feature-c' 2>&1 | head -1)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Build the report.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
{
printf '%.0s#' {1..72}; echo
printf '# %-68s #\n' "JJ ANCESTRY ANALYSIS REPORT"
printf '# %-68s #\n' "Repository : $REPO_DIR"
printf '# %-68s #\n' "Generated  : $(date -u '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S UTC')"
printf '%.0s#' {1..72}; echo

# ---------- Bookmark tips -------------------------------------------------
section "BOOKMARK TIPS"
printf '  %-12s %s\n' "main"      "$MAIN_TIP"
printf '  %-12s %s\n' "feature-a" "$FA_TIP"
printf '  %-12s %s\n' "feature-b" "$FB_TIP"
printf '  %-12s %s\n' "feature-c" "$FC_TIP"

# ---------- Section 1 ------------------------------------------------------
section "1. COMMITS IN feature-a NOT REACHABLE FROM main"
cat <<'DOC'

  Revset: main..feature-a
  Explanation: The range operator X..Y resolves to commits that are ancestors
  of Y (inclusive) but NOT ancestors of X (inclusive).  This is equivalent to
  ancestors(feature-a) ~ ancestors(main) and gives the set of commits that
  are unique to the feature-a line — i.e. work that has not yet been merged
  into main.
DOC
echo
list_commits 'main..feature-a'

# ---------- Section 2 ------------------------------------------------------
section "2. COMMITS IN THE SHARED ANCESTRY OF ALL THREE FEATURE BRANCHES"
cat <<'DOC'

  Revset: ancestors(feature-a) & ancestors(feature-b) & ancestors(feature-c)
  Explanation: ancestors(X) returns every commit reachable by following parent
  edges from X (including X itself).  The intersection (&) keeps only commits
  present in all three ancestor sets — i.e. the common history shared by
  feature-a, feature-b, and feature-c.
DOC
echo
list_commits 'ancestors(feature-a) & ancestors(feature-b) & ancestors(feature-c)'

# ---------- Section 3 ------------------------------------------------------
section "3. MERGE BASE BETWEEN feature-a AND feature-b"
cat <<'DOC'

  Revset: heads(ancestors(feature-a) & ancestors(feature-b))
  Explanation: The merge base is the most-recent common ancestor of two
  branches.  We compute it by intersecting the ancestor sets of the two
  bookmarks and then taking heads() — the subset with no descendants inside
  the set — which yields exactly the deepest common commit(s).
DOC
echo
MERGE_BASE_REVSET='heads(ancestors(feature-a) & ancestors(feature-b))'
list_commits "$MERGE_BASE_REVSET"

echo
echo "  Detailed view:"
echo
jj log \
    --no-pager \
    -T 'indent("    ", short_id ++ " | " ++ committer.email() ++ " | " ++ committer.timestamp().format("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") ++ "\n    " ++ if(description, description.first_line(), "(no description)") ++ "\n\n")' \
    -r "$MERGE_BASE_REVSET" \
    2>&1 || true

# ---------- Section 4 ------------------------------------------------------
section "4. COMMITS THAT MODIFIED src/core.py ACROSS ALL FEATURE BRANCHES"
cat <<'DOC'

  Revset used per branch:
    (main..feature-X) & file("src/core.py")
  Combined:
    (main..feature-a | main..feature-b | main..feature-c) & file("src/core.py")

  Explanation: The file() fileset predicate restricts the revset to commits
  that introduced at least one change to the given path.  We first exclude
  main's history (main..feature-X) so that baseline commits are not counted,
  then intersect with file("src/core.py") to keep only those commits that
  actually touched the file.
DOC
echo

CORE_REVSET='(main..feature-a | main..feature-b | main..feature-c) & file("src/core.py")'

echo "  --- Combined (all feature branches) ---"
list_commits "$CORE_REVSET"

echo
echo "  --- Per-branch breakdown ---"
for branch in feature-a feature-b feature-c; do
    printf '\n  [%s]\n' "$branch"
    list_commits "main..${branch} & file(\"src/core.py\")"
done

echo
echo "  Diff summary for each matching commit:"
echo
while IFS= read -r line; do
    [[ -z "$line" ]] && continue
    short_id="${line%% *}"
    desc="${line#* }"
    printf '  >> %s  %s\n' "$short_id" "$desc"
    jj diff --no-pager -r "$short_id" --stat 2>&1 | sed 's/^/     /'
    echo
done < <(
    jj log \
        --no-graph \
        --no-pager \
        -T 'short_id ++ " " ++ if(description, description.first_line(), "(no description)") ++ "\n"' \
        -r "$CORE_REVSET" \
        2>&1 | grep -v '^$'
)

# ---------- Footer ---------------------------------------------------------
printf '\n%s\n' "$(printf '%.0s#' {1..72})"
printf '# %-68s #\n' "END OF REPORT"
printf '%s\n' "$(printf '%.0s#' {1..72})"

} > "$REPORT" 2>&1

echo "Report written to: $REPORT"

parts.text: The template API uses method syntax (`commit_id.short()`, `description.first_line()`), not standalone keywords. Let me fix the script:

input.searchContent:

    local template="${2:-short_id ++ \" \" ++ if(description, description.first_line(), \"(no description)\") ++ \"\\n\"}"

input.replaceContent:
list_commits() {
    local revset="$1"
    # Template uses method-call syntax supported by this version of jj.
    # commit_id.short() gives the abbreviated hex; description.first_line()
    # gives the subject line, falling back to "(no description)" for empty ones.
    local template='commit_id.short() ++ " " ++ if(description, description.first_line(), "(no description)") ++ "\n"'
    local results
    results=$(
        jj log \
            --no-graph \
            --no-pager \
            -T "$template" \
            -r "$revset" \
            2>&1
    )
    if [[ -z "$results" ]]; then
        echo "  (none)"
    else
        local n=1
        while IFS= read -r line; do
            [[ -z "$line" ]] && continue   # jj emits blank lines between entries
            printf "  %2d. %s\n" "$n" "$line"
            (( n++ ))
        done <<< "$results"
    fi
}

    -T 'indent("    ", commit_id.short() ++ " | " ++ committer.email() ++ " | " ++ committer.timestamp().format("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") ++ "\n    " ++ if(description, description.first_line(), "(no description)") ++ "\n\n")' \
    -r "$MERGE_BASE_REVSET" \
    2>&1 || true

input.searchContent:
while IFS= read -r line; do
    [[ -z "$line" ]] && continue
    short_id="${line%% *}"
    desc="${line#* }"
    printf '  >> %s  %s\n' "$short_id" "$desc"
    jj diff --no-pager -r "$short_id" --stat 2>&1 | sed 's/^/     /'
    echo
done < <(
    jj log \
        --no-graph \
        --no-pager \
        -T 'short_id ++ " " ++ if(description, description.first_line(), "(no description)") ++ "\n"' \
        -r "$CORE_REVSET" \
        2>&1 | grep -v '^$'
)

        --no-graph \

        -T 'commit_id.short() ++ " " ++ if(description, description.first_line(), "(no description)") ++ "\n"' \

)

parts.text: The script is exiting before the redirect to the report file. The `set -e` combined with the output redirection block `{ ... } > "$REPORT"` is the issue — the entire brace-group runs in a subshell-like context with `set -e`, and any command failing inside causes an exit before the redirect completes. Let me check more carefully:

input.command: cd /home/user/repo && bash -x analyze_ancestry.sh 2>/tmp/err.txt ; echo "exit=$?" ; cat /tmp/err.txt | grep -v "^++" | head -80

parts.text: The trace stops right after `FC_TIP` is set — the `{` block isn't even reached. The problem is the `printf '%.0s=' {1..72}` brace expansion: in bash, `{1..72}` inside a heredoc-style subshell or inside the `{ ... }` output-redirect block may interact badly. parts.text: The file has `\r\n` line endings (CRLF) — that's why the script silently fails. The `\r` at the end of each line is being treated as part of the command, causing issues when the shell tries to open the `{` block. Let me fix the line endings:

parts.text: The report IS being created (3550 bytes). The exit code 1 comes from something *after* the redirect block, specifically the `echo "Report written to: $REPORT"` line. Let me check what's in the report and diagnose the exit:

output.output:
########################################################################
# JJ ANCESTRY ANALYSIS REPORT                                          #
# Repository : /home/user/repo                                         #
# Generated  : 2026-03-19 07:26:46 UTC                                 #
########################################################################

========================================================================
 BOOKMARK TIPS
========================================================================
  main         d992ecbc2508
  feature-a    b4e9f7e3c0b0
  feature-b    44037c785806
  feature-c    6ba75fdcd758

========================================================================
 1. COMMITS IN feature-a NOT REACHABLE FROM main
========================================================================

  Revset: main..feature-a
  Explanation: The range operator X..Y resolves to commits that are ancestors
  of Y (inclusive) but NOT ancestors of X (inclusive).  This is equivalent to
  ancestors(feature-a) ~ ancestors(main) and gives the set of commits that
  are unique to the feature-a line — i.e. work that has not yet been merged
  into main.

   1. b4e9f7e3c0b0 feat: feature-a extends core with helper
   2. 6c2feaa8c86a feat: feature-a adds dedicated module
   3. bd4b5c3f8966 feat: feature-a adds core function

========================================================================
 2. COMMITS IN THE SHARED ANCESTRY OF ALL THREE FEATURE BRANCHES
========================================================================

  Revset: ancestors(feature-a) & ancestors(feature-b) & ancestors(feature-c)
  Explanation: ancestors(X) returns every commit reachable by following parent
  edges from X (including X itself).  The intersection (&) keeps only commits
  present in all three ancestor sets — i.e. the common history shared by
  feature-a, feature-b, and feature-c.

   1. d992ecbc2508 chore: add config module
   2. d02c9f70f976 chore: add utils module
   3. a00e3fdf567a chore: initial project setup
   4. 000000000000 (no description)

========================================================================
 3. MERGE BASE BETWEEN feature-a AND feature-b
========================================================================

  Revset: heads(ancestors(feature-a) & ancestors(feature-b))
  Explanation: The merge base is the most-recent common ancestor of two
  branches.  We compute it by intersecting the ancestor sets of the two
  bookmarks and then taking heads() — the subset with no descendants inside
  the set — which yields exactly the deepest common commit(s).

   1. d992ecbc2508 chore: add config module

  Detailed view:

○      d992ecbc2508 | test@example.com | 2026-03-11 12:33:36
│          chore: add config module
~

========================================================================
 4. COMMITS THAT MODIFIED src/core.py ACROSS ALL FEATURE BRANCHES
========================================================================

  Revset used per branch:
    (main..feature-X) & file("src/core.py")
  Combined:
    (main..feature-a | main..feature-b | main..feature-c) & file("src/core.py")

  Explanation: The file() fileset predicate restricts the revset to commits
  that introduced at least one change to the given path.  We first exclude
  main's history (main..feature-X) so that baseline commits are not counted,
  then intersect with file("src/core.py") to keep only those commits that
  actually touched the file.

  --- Combined (all feature branches) ---

CORE_REVSET='(main..feature-a | main..feature-b | main..feature-c) & file("src/core.py")'

echo

    list_commits "main..${branch} & file(\"src/core.py\")"
done

parts.text: The report is complete and correct. Let me do one small fix — the Section 4 header prose still says `file()` (the old name); update it to `files()`:

input.searchContent:
cat <<'DOC'

  Revset used per branch:
    (main..feature-X) & file("src/core.py")
  Combined:
    (main..feature-a | main..feature-b | main..feature-c) & file("src/core.py")

  Explanation: The file() fileset predicate restricts the revset to commits
  that introduced at least one change to the given path.  We first exclude
  main's history (main..feature-X) so that baseline commits are not counted,
  then intersect with file("src/core.py") to keep only those commits that
  actually touched the file.
DOC
input.replaceContent:
cat <<'DOC'

  Revset used per branch:
    (main..feature-X) & files("src/core.py")
  Combined:
    (main..feature-a | main..feature-b | main..feature-c) & files("src/core.py")

  Explanation: The files() fileset predicate restricts the revset to commits
  that introduced at least one change to the given path.  We first exclude
  main's history (main..feature-X) so that baseline commits are not counted,
  then intersect with files("src/core.py") to keep only those commits that
  actually touched the file.
DOC

input.command:
python3 -c "
content = open('/home/user/repo/analyze_ancestry.sh', 'rb').read()
content = content.replace(b'\r\n', b'\n').replace(b'\r', b'\n')
open('/home/user/repo/analyze_ancestry.sh', 'wb').write(content)
" && cd /home/user/repo && bash analyze_ancestry.sh && echo "exit=$?" && cat /home/user/ancestry_report.txt

output.output:
Report written to: /home/user/ancestry_report.txt
exit=0
########################################################################
# JJ ANCESTRY ANALYSIS REPORT                                          #
# Repository : /home/user/repo                                         #
# Generated  : 2026-03-19 07:27:45 UTC                                 #
########################################################################

========================================================================
 BOOKMARK TIPS
========================================================================
  main         d992ecbc2508
  feature-a    b4e9f7e3c0b0
  feature-b    44037c785806
  feature-c    6ba75fdcd758

========================================================================
 1. COMMITS IN feature-a NOT REACHABLE FROM main
========================================================================

  Revset: main..feature-a
  Explanation: The range operator X..Y resolves to commits that are ancestors
  of Y (inclusive) but NOT ancestors of X (inclusive).  This is equivalent to
  ancestors(feature-a) ~ ancestors(main) and gives the set of commits that
  are unique to the feature-a line — i.e. work that has not yet been merged
  into main.

   1. b4e9f7e3c0b0 feat: feature-a extends core with helper
   2. 6c2feaa8c86a feat: feature-a adds dedicated module
   3. bd4b5c3f8966 feat: feature-a adds core function

========================================================================
 2. COMMITS IN THE SHARED ANCESTRY OF ALL THREE FEATURE BRANCHES
========================================================================

  Revset: ancestors(feature-a) & ancestors(feature-b) & ancestors(feature-c)
  Explanation: ancestors(X) returns every commit reachable by following parent
  edges from X (including X itself).  The intersection (&) keeps only commits
  present in all three ancestor sets — i.e. the common history shared by
  feature-a, feature-b, and feature-c.

   1. d992ecbc2508 chore: add config module
   2. d02c9f70f976 chore: add utils module
   3. a00e3fdf567a chore: initial project setup
   4. 000000000000 (no description)

========================================================================
 3. MERGE BASE BETWEEN feature-a AND feature-b
========================================================================

  Revset: heads(ancestors(feature-a) & ancestors(feature-b))
  Explanation: The merge base is the most-recent common ancestor of two
  branches.  We compute it by intersecting the ancestor sets of the two
  bookmarks and then taking heads() — the subset with no descendants inside
  the set — which yields exactly the deepest common commit(s).

   1. d992ecbc2508 chore: add config module

  Detailed view:

○      d992ecbc2508 | test@example.com | 2026-03-11 12:33:36
│          chore: add config module
~

========================================================================
 4. COMMITS THAT MODIFIED src/core.py ACROSS ALL FEATURE BRANCHES
========================================================================

  Revset used per branch:
    (main..feature-X) & files("src/core.py")
  Combined:
    (main..feature-a | main..feature-b | main..feature-c) & files("src/core.py")

  Explanation: The files() fileset predicate restricts the revset to commits
  that introduced at least one change to the given path.  We first exclude
  main's history (main..feature-X) so that baseline commits are not counted,
  then intersect with files("src/core.py") to keep only those commits that
  actually touched the file.

  --- Combined (all feature branches) ---

   2. 44037c785806 feat: feature-b modifies core
   3. b4e9f7e3c0b0 feat: feature-a extends core with helper

  [feature-a]

   2. bd4b5c3f8966 feat: feature-a adds core function

  [feature-b]

todos.content: Write the jj ancestry analysis script
Run the script and verify the output report

input.result:
The script `/home/user/repo/analyze_ancestry.sh` is written and the report has been generated at `/home/user/ancestry_report.txt`. Here's what was built and found:

---

### Script: `analyze_ancestry.sh`

The script uses four distinct `jj` revsets to answer each question:

| Section | Revset | Operator explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `main..feature-a` | `X..Y` = ancestors(Y) minus ancestors(X); commits exclusive to feature-a |
| 2 | `ancestors(feature-a) & ancestors(feature-b) & ancestors(feature-c)` | Set intersection (`&`) of all three ancestor sets = shared history |
| 3 | `heads(ancestors(feature-a) & ancestors(feature-b))` | `heads()` on the intersection gives the deepest common commit(s) = merge base |
| 4 | `(main..feature-a \| main..feature-b \| main..feature-c) & files("src/core.py")` | Union of feature ranges intersected with the `files()` fileset predicate |

---

### Report findings (`/home/user/ancestry_report.txt`)

**§1 — Commits in `feature-a` not reachable from `main`** (3 commits):
- `bd4b5c3f` feat: feature-a adds core function
- `6c2feaa8` feat: feature-a adds dedicated module
- `b4e9f7e3` feat: feature-a extends core with helper

**§2 — Shared ancestry of all three feature branches** (4 commits — the entire `main` lineage):
- `d992ecbc` chore: add config module
- `d02c9f70` chore: add utils module
- `a00e3fdf` chore: initial project setup
- `00000000` (root)

**§3 — Merge base between `feature-a` and `feature-b`**:
- `d992ecbc` chore: add config module — this is the tip of `main`, the most recent common ancestor of both feature branches.

**§4 — Commits touching `src/core.py` across all feature branches** (4 commits, per-branch breakdown included):
- `bd4b5c3f` (feature-a) — adds core function
- `b4e9f7e3` (feature-a / feature-c) — extends core with helper
- `44037c78` (feature-b) — modifies core
- `6ba75fdc` (feature-c) — modifies core too

Each section includes the revset expression, a plain-English explanation of the operators used, and a numbered commit list. Section 4 also includes a per-branch breakdown and `jj diff --stat` output for each matching commit.

Re-run any time with `bash /home/user/repo/analyze_ancestry.sh` — the report is fully regenerated each run.
